documents.doc_description: The **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Know Your Customer) Directions, 2025** (RBI/DOR/2025-26/169; DOR.AML.REC.No.88/14.01.002/2025-26), **updated as on December 29, 2025**, lays down the RBI’s KYC/AML/CFT regulatory framework **applicable to commercial banks**. It covers governance expectations (Board-approved KYC/AML policies and general guidelines), **risk management**, and detailed operational requirements for **Customer Identification Procedure (CIP)** and **Customer Due Diligence (CDD)** for individuals, sole proprietorships and legal entities, including **beneficial ownership identification**, ongoing due diligence, and **enhanced/simplified due diligence**. The Directions also prescribe **record management**, **reporting to FIU-IND**, obligations arising from **international agreements/communications from international agencies**, and other instructions. They **commence from the date specified in the Directions (issued November 28, 2025)** and include **repeal/saving and interpretation provisions**, with amendments reflected up to **December 29, 2025**.
top_topics: exclusion sfb payment banks local area banks

documents.doc_description: **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Asset Liability Management) Directions, 2025** (RBI/DOR/2025-26/163 dated November 28, 2025) lay down the RBI’s regulatory framework for asset-liability management, primarily for **commercial banks** to which the Directions are made applicable. The Directions set out **Board and senior management governance** expectations, including approval and periodic review of ALM policies, limits and oversight arrangements. They prescribe detailed requirements for **liquidity risk management**, covering risk identification, measurement (flow and stock approaches), monitoring and MIS, liquidity across currencies, risk tolerance, internal controls, off-balance sheet/contingent exposures, collateral management, internal funds transfer pricing, diversified funding strategy, intra-group liquidity risks, stress testing, and contingency funding planning, including for overseas operations. They also include a dedicated chapter on **intraday liquidity management**; the Directions **commence from the date specified under “Short Title and Commencement” (November 28, 2025)**, and any subsequent amendments would be issued by RBI through further circulars/directions.
top_topics: financial statements presentation and disclosures directions 2025

documents.doc_name: Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Internal Ombudsman) Directions,.pdf
documents.doc_description: The **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Internal Ombudsman) Directions, 2026** (issued under **Section 35A of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949**) prescribe a strengthened internal grievance redress framework through an **Internal Ombudsman (IO)** mechanism. They apply to **commercial banks** having **10 or more banking outlets in India as on March 31, 2025** (and to banks that meet this threshold later, **six months after** meeting it), with specified exclusions as per the definition of “commercial banks.” The Directions cover the **establishment and functioning of the IO/Dy. IO office**, definitions of complaints/deficiency in service/customer, and processes to ensure **review of complaints before rejection** by an apex-level authority within the bank for faster, meaningful resolution. They are **effective immediately from January 14, 2026**, except **clause 7(2), 14(2) and 14(4)** which must be complied with **by June 30, 2026**, and they also provide RBI a power to **suspend** provisions (and extend such suspension) for specified periods/entities.
top_topics: customer service committee of the board

documents.doc_name: Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Securitisation Transactions) Directions, 2025.pdf
documents.doc_description: The **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Securitisation Transactions) Directions, 2025** (RBI/DOR/2025-26/160 dated **November 28, 2025**) lay down the regulatory framework for **securitisation transactions undertaken by commercial banks** in India, including banks acting as **originators, sponsors, service providers, or investors** in securitisation exposures. The Directions cover **eligibility of assets for securitisation**, **minimum retention requirement (MRR)**, **origination standards**, **payment waterfall/observability**, limits on retained exposures, **issuance and listing**, **SPE conditions**, **representations and warranties**, and **accounting provisions**. They also prescribe criteria and oversight for **Simple, Transparent and Comparable (STC) securitisations**, rules for banks providing **credit enhancement, liquidity, underwriting and servicing facilities**, and investor-side requirements such as **due diligence, stress testing, and ongoing monitoring/valuation**. The framework further sets out **capital requirements for securitisation exposures and related disclosure norms**; the provided extract does not specify the **effective date/commencement clause or subsequent amendments**, which would be contained in Chapter I.

documents.doc_name: Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Digital Banking Channels.pdf
documents.doc_description: The **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Digital Banking Channels Authorisation) Directions, 2025** (RBI/DOR/2025-26/380 dated **November 28, 2025**) lay down the regulatory framework for **authorisation and operation of digital banking channels by commercial banks** in India. They apply to **commercial banks** (as defined in the Directions) offering or proposing to offer digital banking channels, and set out **prudential requirements** for such activities. The Directions also prescribe **technology-related guidelines** for digital banking (including governance, controls and risk management expectations) and provide **general operational guidelines** for compliance and oversight. The document includes provisions on **exemptions, repeal and other consequential matters**; the **effective date and any subsequent amendments** would be as specified in the operative provisions and RBI updates to these Directions.

documents.doc_name: Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Climate Finance and Management.pdf
documents.doc_description: The **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Climate Finance and Management of Climate Change Risks) Directions, 2025** (RBI/DOR/2025-26/172 dated **November 28, 2025**) apply to **commercial banks** as specified under the Directions’ applicability provisions. They set out governance expectations for the **Board’s role** in overseeing climate finance and climate risk management, and prescribe a detailed **framework for acceptance of green deposits**, including policy requirements, deposit features (denomination/interest/tenor), a financing framework, **use of proceeds**, **third‑party verification/assurance**, **impact assessment**, and **reporting and disclosures**. The Directions take effect from the **commencement date stated in Chapter I**, and include **repeal and saving** provisions along with clarifications on the application of other laws and interpretation (Chapter IV).

documents.doc_name: Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks - Resource Raising Norms).pdf
documents.doc_description: The **Reserve Bank of India (Commercial Banks – Resource Raising Norms) Directions, 2025** (RBI/DoR/2025-26/152 dated **November 28, 2025**) apply to **commercial banks** as specified under the Directions’ applicability clause. They lay down the regulatory framework for **raising resources through the issue of long-term bonds specifically for financing infrastructure sub-sectors**, including conditions and compliance expectations around such issuances. The Directions also contain **repeal and saving provisions**, clarify that **other applicable laws continue to apply**, and provide **interpretation** guidance. They **come into force from the date of commencement stated in Chapter I**, and **repeal the earlier corresponding RBI instructions on this subject to the extent specified**, with savings for actions already taken.

relationships.description: Both instruments are RBI Directions applicable to commercial banks and set governance/operational expectations for bank-wide compliance frameworks (though on different subjects: grievance redress/Internal Ombudsman vs KYC/AML). No explicit cross-citation is evident from the provided summary.

relationships.description: Both are RBI Directions aimed at commercial banks with Board/senior management oversight expectations, but they regulate different domains (Internal Ombudsman/grievance redress vs asset-liability management). No explicit reference/amendment/supersession is evident from the provided summary.

relationships.description: Both documents regulate commercial banks and have overlapping prudential risk-management themes. The Securitisation Directions, 2025 include requirements around liquidity/credit enhancement facilities, investor stress testing, and ongoing monitoring of securitisation exposures, which intersect with the ALM Directions’ broader liquidity risk management, stress testing, and treatment of off-balance-sheet/contingent exposures.
relationships.evidence: NEW DOC: "rules for banks providing credit enhancement, liquidity, underwriting and servicing facilities" and "investor-side requirements such as due diligence, stress testing, and ongoing monitoring/valuation". EXISTING DOC (ALM Directions, 2025): "liquidity risk management... off-balance sheet/contingent exposures... stress testing".
